items.q: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING in SQL?
items.correct: WHERE filters individual rows before any grouping; HAVING filters the groups produced by GROUP BY, typically using aggregate values.
items.misconception: They are interchangeable - both filter rows at any point.
items.random_wrong: HAVING is just the British spelling of WHERE.
items.why: Both express conditions, so students swap them. Order of operations is the key: rows are filtered, then grouped, then groups are filtered - WHERE first, HAVING after.

items.q: Does DELETE FROM Students remove the Students table?
items.correct: No - DELETE removes rows (all of them, without a WHERE clause) but the empty table and its structure remain; DROP TABLE removes the table itself.
items.misconception: Yes - DELETE and DROP both erase the table completely.
items.random_wrong: Yes - DELETE also removes the whole database file.
items.why: The verbs feel synonymous. Distinguish content from container: DELETE empties, DROP demolishes - an empty table can still be queried, a dropped one cannot.

items.q: Is SELECT * the required way to start every query?
items.correct: No - the asterisk is shorthand for all columns; naming only the columns you need is usually better practice, reducing transferred data and ambiguity.
items.misconception: Yes - SQL syntax demands the asterisk in every SELECT.
items.random_wrong: Yes - SELECT without * returns no data at all.
items.why: Tutorial examples overuse the wildcard until it looks mandatory. The column list is the real syntax; * is a convenience with performance costs.

items.q: Which SQL keyword filters rows according to a condition?
items.why: Correct answer: WHERE. “ORDER” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: An SQL JOIN is used to:
items.correct: Combine rows from two tables by matching key values
items.why: It's Combine rows from two tables by matching key values. “Delete two tables at once” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: COUNT, SUM and AVG are examples of:
items.correct: Aggregate functions that summarise many rows into one value
items.why: It's Aggregate functions that summarise many rows into one value. “Data types” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the difference between DELETE and DROP TABLE?
items.correct: DELETE removes rows from a table; DROP TABLE removes the entire table and its structure
items.misconception: They do exactly the same thing
items.why: It's DELETE removes rows from a table; DROP TABLE removes the entire table and its structure. “They do exactly the same thing” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Table A has 4 rows and table B has 3 rows. How many rows does their Cartesian product (CROSS JOIN) contain?
items.why: Exact answer: 12 rows. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
